perturbation.original_text: Agent or its successors shall maintain during the term of this Agreement and while it is still responsible for its Book of Business: i.All required workers' compensation or similar insurance; ii.Comprehensive general liability insurance. Agent shall promptly supply Kallo with proof of such insurance upon request. Kallo shall maintain comprehensive general liability and errors and omissions insurance for the Kallo Mobile care suite of products. Kallo shall promptly supply Agent with proof of such insurance upon request.
perturbation.changed_text: Agent or its successors shall maintain liability insurance during the term of this Agreement and while it is still responsible for its Book of Business. Agent shall supply Kallo with proof of such insurance upon request. Kallo shall maintain errors and omissions insurance for the Kallo Mobile care suite of products. Kallo shall supply Agent with proof of such insurance upon request.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'all required workers' compensation or similar insurance' and 'comprehensive general liability insurance' for the Agent and 'comprehensive general liability' for Kallo, the contract fails to ensure adequate coverage, potentially contradicting mandatory insurance requirements under Nigerian law, where the agreement is focused.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Labour Act, CAP L1, Laws of the Federation of Nigeria 2004
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 69(1) of the Labour Act mandates employers to provide compensation for injuries sustained by employees during their employment. Removing the explicit requirement for worker's compensation insurance could leave the agent exposed to liabilities they cannot cover, effectively contradicting the intent of the law to protect workers.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NATLEX - - Skip to Main Content Home Breadcrumb Title_of_text Main Region First Region Country(ies) Adopted on (Date of text)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes explicit requirements for workers' compensation and comprehensive general liability insurance for the Agent, and comprehensive general liability insurance for Kallo. The law explanation indicates that Section 69(1) of the Labour Act mandates employers to provide compensation for injuries sustained by employees. Removing the explicit requirement for worker's compensation insurance, as evidenced by the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, makes the contract non-compliant and creates a legal loophole, contradicting the intent of the law to protect workers. The omission undermines a legally mandated protection, as stipulated by the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition. While the scraped snippets are minimal, the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation, combined with the explicit removal of insurance requirements, support the conclusion that this omission constitutes a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The pricing given in Schedule A is the transfer price from Kallo to the Agent and the Agent shall, in its discretion establish a reasonable price for each of the Kallo Mobile care suite of products EULA as per Schedule A. For Maintenance Contract pricing and details refer to Schedule A, section (9), subparagraph (B). Kallo reserves the right to revise and republish prices as shown in Schedule A from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: The pricing given in Schedule A is the transfer price from Kallo to the Agent and the Agent shall, in its discretion establish a price for each of the Kallo Mobile care suite of products EULA as per Schedule A. Kallo reserves the right to revise and republish prices as shown in Schedule A from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'reasonable', the contract allows the agent to set prices that may be exploitative and unconscionable. This removes a critical consumer protection element. Nigerian law protects consumers from unfair trade practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Competition and Consumer Protection Act, 2018
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 115(1) of the Federal Competition and Consumer Protection Act prohibits unfair, unreasonable, inequitable, or unjust contract terms. Removing the 'reasonable' pricing constraint potentially violates this section by enabling exploitative pricing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Introduction Of Country-By-Country Reporting In Curaςao - Accounting Standards - Netherlands ARTICLE 25 October 2018 Introduction Of Country-By-Country Reporting In Curaςao TG TMF Group BV More Contributor TMF Group experts work from 120 offices in 80+ jurisdictions, making sure that complex administrative tasks are done right and on time. From legal set-up and oversight to regulatory filings, accounting, tax and payroll, we look after our clients’ administrative burdens so they can focus on their businesses. Explore Firm Details This law has been introduced retroactively for fiscal year 2018 so all companies must comply in 2019. Netherlands Accounting and Audit Evert Rakers Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. Curaςao's Parliament has adopted legislation on 7 June 2018 stating that additional documentation is required by Curaςao entities that qualify for Country-by-Country reporting ("CbCR"). In order to be compliant with this new law, multinational companies might be obliged to provide a country- by country report, a master file and a local file. This law has been introduced retroactively for fiscal year 2018 so all companies must comply in 2019. Country by Country reporting These tax law changes will help Curaςao to stay compliant with the regulations of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD). This reporting will also help with transparency about possible profit shifting in multinational structures. If an entity in Curaçao is the ultimate corporate parent of multinational enterprise that operates in another jurisdiction besides Curaçao then complying with CbCR is mandatory. This requirement only applies to a multinational enterprise with consolidated revenues exceeding ANG 1,500,000,000 or approximately EUR 750,000,000. The CbCR filing must include information (specified in the law) about every jurisdiction where the company is active. It must be provided within 12 months from the last day of each financial year. Also, if the ultimate parent is not required to file the documentation in its jurisdiction and the multinational enterprise has not assigned another surrogate ultimate parent to file elsewhere, then the Curaçao entity must file the documentation. Master and local file Part of the new law concerns the master file and local file that needs to be available for the Curaςao Tax Department. The requirements for this type of reporting is also based on consolidated revenues of a multinational entity. Any Curaçao entity that forms part of a multinational structure with consolidated revenues exceeding ANG 100,000,000 (or EUR 50,000,000) must prepare a master file and a local file. The master file must contain general information about transfer pricing of the multinational enterprise. The local file must contain detailed information about intergroup transactions which a local entity participates in. Penalties If the report is not provided or incomplete, there can be penalties for companies. The fines range from ANG 100,000 (EUR 50,000) up to ANG 250,000 (EUR 125,000). How can we help? Many countries around the globe are enacting similar legislation so it is important for multinational companies to understand the specific regulations for Curaςao. While the information required may be similar, it is always a complex process when reporting for various entities. These new laws are another set of compliance obligations that companies in Curaςao must abide by. It is important to partner with local experts that can help with filing the CbCR and the master and local file. TMF Curaçao has the experts that understand the complexities of local laws, regulations, processes, and requirements. To find out how we can support your company through these
perturbation.justification: The original text included the term 'reasonable price,' providing a consumer protection element that the modified text removes. The Law Explanation states that Section 115(1) of the Federal Competition and Consumer Protection Act prohibits unfair, unreasonable, inequitable, or unjust contract terms. By removing 'reasonable', the modified text allows for potentially exploitative pricing, directly contradicting the intent of Section 115(1). The Perturbation Explanation underscores that removing 'reasonable' allows for potentially exploitative and unconscionable prices, violating consumer protection. Although neither law snippet is related, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both agree on the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Kallo may directly, or through its Agent at any time during normal business hours, upon no less than 10 business days' notice, and for any reason inspect such records and other financial information relevant to Kallo Mobile care suite of products as sold by Agent to its end users, solely for the purpose of verifying amounts due under this Agreement. Any such information will be confidential information of Agent subject to the provisions of Section 4. Warranties:
perturbation.changed_text: Kallo may directly, or through its Agent at any time inspect such records and other financial information relevant to Kallo Mobile care suite of products as sold by Agent to its end users. Any such information will be confidential information of Agent subject to the provisions of Section 4. Warranties:
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'during normal business hours, upon no less than 10 business days' notice, and for any reason solely for the purpose of verifying amounts due under this Agreement' restriction, the contract allows Kallo to conduct arbitrary and unannounced inspections of the agent's financial records, violating privacy and potentially contradicting laws protecting business confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 37 of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria guarantees the right to privacy of citizens, their homes, correspondence, telephone conversations and telegraphic communications. Removing the notice and reasonable cause requirements for inspecting the Agent's records can be interpreted as a violation of this right to privacy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text contains restrictions on when and why Kallo may inspect the agent's records, namely 'during normal business hours, upon no less than 10 business days' notice, and for any reason solely for the purpose of verifying amounts due under this Agreement'. The changed text removes these restrictions. The perturbation explanation states that this removal violates privacy and potentially contradicts laws protecting business confidentiality. The Law Explanation mentions the right to privacy. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unhelpful, the removal of the restrictions in the changed text results in a clear reduction in the agent's protection, thus creating a legal contradiction as the change undermines the original legal intent.
